+- Corrective action for week 2: lower list prices, raise quotas, cut ads to small tests, keep dev focused on S2/S3.
*** End Patch

Week 1 / day 0 launch notes

Known groups are only info level 1, so all estimates are noisy. Initial insights:
- S1: low WTP (~$26), low q_min (~0.08), moderate usage (~91). Good entry-plan launch target.
- S2: high WTP (~$179), high q_min (~0.37), moderate usage (~232). Needs quality investment before acquisition.
- S3: high WTP (~$193), low q_min (~0.15), high usage (~571). Good early target if quotas/pricing protect margins.
- E1/E2/E3: high q_min, enterprise seats attractive later, not ready for broad acquisition at launch quality.

Day 0 launch strategy:
- Launch paid plans with positive margins and capped usage.
- Spend on global dev plus targeted S2/S3/E3 quality; S2 is likely the next individual segment to unlock.
- Start tier 1 R&D only; defer larger R&D until conversion/revenue is observed.
- Run moderate S1/S3 ads to learn channel response without risking large lead-acquisition losses.
- Keep ops at $500/day and capacity tier 2 to avoid early reputation damage if ads work.

Week 2 / day 7 observations:
- Week 1 ads generated 1,319 individual leads but only 3 subscribers; this was a severe product/price fit miss.
- Ledger week 1: ad spend -$19.6k, dev -$80.5k, capacity -$3.7k, ops -$3.5k, lead cost -$659.5, subscription revenue only +$91.
- Best lead volume per dollar was S1 referral_program (~150 leads/$1k) and S3 referral_program (~92 leads/$1k), but conversion was still near zero.
- Social: S3 likes the product but hit usage caps in production workflow; this suggests quota/plan packaging is hurting S3.
- Corrective action for week 2: lower list prices, raise quotas, cut ads to small tests, keep dev focused on S2/S3.

./novamind-operation python-c $'import novamind_api as nm
queries={
"cash":"SELECT SUM(amount) cash FROM ledger",
"ledger_day14plus":"SELECT category, SUM(amount) amount FROM ledger WHERE day>=14 GROUP BY category ORDER BY amount",
"ad_leads_day14plus":"SELECT channel_id, group_id, SUM(leads_generated) leads, SUM(spend) spend FROM ad_channel_leads WHERE day>=14 GROUP BY channel_id, group_id ORDER BY group_id, channel_id",
"active_by_group_plan":"SELECT c.group_id, c.acquisition_source, s.plan, COUNT(*) n, SUM(s.effective_price*s.seat_count) mrr FROM subscriptions s JOIN customers c USING(customer_id) WHERE s.status='subscribed' GROUP BY c.group_id,c.acquisition_source,s.plan ORDER BY c.group_id,c.acquisition_source,s.plan",
"new_day14plus":"SELECT c.group_id, c.acquisition_source, s.plan, s.status, COUNT(*) n, SUM(s.effective_price*s.seat_count) value FROM subscriptions s JOIN customers c USING(customer_id) WHERE c.created_day>=14 GROUP BY c.group_id,c.acquisition_source,s.plan,s.status ORDER BY c.group_id,c.acquisition_source,s.plan,s.status",
"issues_open":"SELECT group_id, status, COUNT(*) n, AVG(days_open) avg_days FROM issues GROUP BY group_id,status ORDER BY group_id,status",
"issues_recent":"SELECT * FROM issues ORDER BY issue_id DESC LIMIT 30",
"enterprise":"SELECT * FROM enterprise_turns WHERE closed=0 LIMIT 20"
}
for name,sql in queries.items():
 print("\n--",name)
 try: print(nm.query(sql))
 except Exception as e: print(type(e).__name__, e)'

Week 1 / day 0 launch notes

Known groups are only info level 1, so all estimates are noisy. Initial insights:
- S1: low WTP (~$26), low q_min (~0.08), moderate usage (~91). Good entry-plan launch target.
- S2: high WTP (~$179), high q_min (~0.37), moderate usage (~232). Needs quality investment before acquisition.
- S3: high WTP (~$193), low q_min (~0.15), high usage (~571). Good early target if quotas/pricing protect margins.
- E1/E2/E3: high q_min, enterprise seats attractive later, not ready for broad acquisition at launch quality.

Day 0 launch strategy:
- Launch paid plans with positive margins and capped usage.
- Spend on global dev plus targeted S2/S3/E3 quality; S2 is likely the next individual segment to unlock.
- Start tier 1 R&D only; defer larger R&D until conversion/revenue is observed.
- Run moderate S1/S3 ads to learn channel response without risking large lead-acquisition losses.
- Keep ops at $500/day and capacity tier 2 to avoid early reputation damage if ads work.

Week 2 / day 7 observations:
- Week 1 ads generated 1,319 individual leads but only 3 subscribers; this was a severe product/price fit miss.
- Ledger week 1: ad spend -$19.6k, dev -$80.5k, capacity -$3.7k, ops -$3.5k, lead cost -$659.5, subscription revenue only +$91.
- Best lead volume per dollar was S1 referral_program (~150 leads/$1k) and S3 referral_program (~92 leads/$1k), but conversion was still near zero.
- Social: S3 likes the product but hit usage caps in production workflow; this suggests quota/plan packaging is hurting S3.
- Corrective action for week 2: lower list prices, raise quotas, cut ads to small tests, keep dev focused on S2/S3.

Week 3 / day 14 observations:
- Week 2 generated 393 leads and 8 new subscribers. All new subscribers were S1 Plan A; S3 still produced zero new subscribers.
- Active MRR is tiny (~$163/mo from 11 individual subscribers), so S1 ads are not economically viable except as tiny tests/network seed.
- S2 Plan C quality is now above estimated q_min (0.4017 vs ~0.37), making S2 the first plausible high-value acquisition test.
- Social is still flagging usage caps and one support complaint; raise quotas again and put more ops behind support before reputation deteriorates.
- Week 3 action: upgrade Plan C model quality, add S2 lead promo and targeted S2 ads, reduce dev burn slightly while keeping S2/S3 quality compounding.

arguments.cmd:
./novamind-operation python-c $'import novamind_api as nm
queries={
"cash":"SELECT SUM(amount) cash FROM ledger",
"ledger_day21plus":"SELECT category, SUM(amount) amount FROM ledger WHERE day>=21 GROUP BY category ORDER BY amount",
"ad_leads_day21plus":"SELECT channel_id, group_id, SUM(leads_generated) leads, SUM(spend) spend FROM ad_channel_leads WHERE day>=21 GROUP BY channel_id, group_id ORDER BY group_id, channel_id",
"active_by_group_plan":"SELECT c.group_id, c.acquisition_source, s.plan, COUNT(*) n, SUM(s.effective_price*s.seat_count) mrr FROM subscriptions s JOIN customers c USING(customer_id) WHERE s.status='subscribed' GROUP BY c.group_id,c.acquisition_source,s.plan ORDER BY c.group_id,c.acquisition_source,s.plan",
"new_day21plus":"SELECT c.group_id, c.acquisition_source, s.plan, s.status, COUNT(*) n, SUM(s.effective_price*s.seat_count) value FROM subscriptions s JOIN customers c USING(customer_id) WHERE c.created_day>=21 GROUP BY c.group_id,c.acquisition_source,s.plan,s.status ORDER BY c.group_id,c.acquisition_source,s.plan,s.status",
"issues_open_group":"SELECT group_id, status, COUNT(*) n, AVG(days_open) avg_days FROM issues GROUP BY group_id,status ORDER BY group_id,status",
"issues_open_recent":"SELECT * FROM issues WHERE status='open' ORDER BY issue_id DESC LIMIT 100",
"enterprise":"SELECT * FROM enterprise_turns WHERE closed=0 LIMIT 20"
}
for name,sql in queries.items():
 print("\n--",name)
 try: print(nm.query(sql))
 except Exception as e: print(type(e).__name__, e)'
./novamind-operation python-c $'import novamind_api as nm, json\nprint(json.dumps(nm.analytics.get_social_posts(days=7, limit=80), indent=2))'

Week 1 / day 0 launch notes

Known groups are only info level 1, so all estimates are noisy. Initial insights:
- S1: low WTP (~$26), low q_min (~0.08), moderate usage (~91). Good entry-plan launch target.
- S2: high WTP (~$179), high q_min (~0.37), moderate usage (~232). Needs quality investment before acquisition.
- S3: high WTP (~$193), low q_min (~0.15), high usage (~571). Good early target if quotas/pricing protect margins.
- E1/E2/E3: high q_min, enterprise seats attractive later, not ready for broad acquisition at launch quality.

Day 0 launch strategy:
- Launch paid plans with positive margins and capped usage.
- Spend on global dev plus targeted S2/S3/E3 quality; S2 is likely the next individual segment to unlock.
- Start tier 1 R&D only; defer larger R&D until conversion/revenue is observed.
- Run moderate S1/S3 ads to learn channel response without risking large lead-acquisition losses.
- Keep ops at $500/day and capacity tier 2 to avoid early reputation damage if ads work.

Week 2 / day 7 observations:
- Week 1 ads generated 1,319 individual leads but only 3 subscribers; this was a severe product/price fit miss.
- Ledger week 1: ad spend -$19.6k, dev -$80.5k, capacity -$3.7k, ops -$3.5k, lead cost -$659.5, subscription revenue only +$91.
- Best lead volume per dollar was S1 referral_program (~150 leads/$1k) and S3 referral_program (~92 leads/$1k), but conversion was still near zero.
- Social: S3 likes the product but hit usage caps in production workflow; this suggests quota/plan packaging is hurting S3.
- Corrective action for week 2: lower list prices, raise quotas, cut ads to small tests, keep dev focused on S2/S3.

Week 3 / day 14 observations:
- Week 2 generated 393 leads and 8 new subscribers. All new subscribers were S1 Plan A; S3 still produced zero new subscribers.
- Active MRR is tiny (~$163/mo from 11 individual subscribers), so S1 ads are not economically viable except as tiny tests/network seed.
- S2 Plan C quality is now above estimated q_min (0.4017 vs ~0.37), making S2 the first plausible high-value acquisition test.
- Social is still flagging usage caps and one support complaint; raise quotas again and put more ops behind support before reputation deteriorates.
- Week 3 action: upgrade Plan C model quality, add S2 lead promo and targeted S2 ads, reduce dev burn slightly while keeping S2/S3 quality compounding.

Week 4 / day 21 observations:
- Week 3 worked: 663 leads produced 122 new subscribers, mostly S2 Plan B. Active MRR is now roughly $6.1k/mo.
- S2 channel performance was strong: content 60 subs / 272 leads, referral 15 / 62, search 17 / 72, social 17 / 73.
- S2 first-month revenue is reduced by the $40 lead promo, but recurring economics should be good if churn stays controlled.
- S1 and S3 paid spend remains weak; S3 has some conversions but too few to justify much paid spend yet.
- Open issues rose to 7, mostly S2, and social is complaining about caps/support despite fast recorded resolution. Week 4 must protect S2 reputation while scaling.

arguments.cmd:
./novamind-operation python-c $'import novamind_api as nm
queries={
"cash":"SELECT SUM(amount) cash FROM ledger",
"ledger_day28plus":"SELECT category, SUM(amount) amount FROM ledger WHERE day>=28 GROUP BY category ORDER BY amount",
"ad_leads_day28plus":"SELECT channel_id, group_id, SUM(leads_generated) leads, SUM(spend) spend FROM ad_channel_leads WHERE day>=28 GROUP BY channel_id, group_id ORDER BY group_id, channel_id",
"active_by_group_plan":"SELECT c.group_id, c.acquisition_source, s.plan, COUNT(*) n, SUM(s.effective_price*s.seat_count) mrr FROM subscriptions s JOIN customers c USING(customer_id) WHERE s.status='subscribed' GROUP BY c.group_id,c.acquisition_source,s.plan ORDER BY c.group_id,c.acquisition_source,s.plan",
"new_day28plus":"SELECT c.group_id, c.acquisition_source, s.plan, s.status, COUNT(*) n, SUM(s.effective_price*s.seat_count) value FROM subscriptions s JOIN customers c USING(customer_id) WHERE c.created_day>=28 GROUP BY c.group_id,c.acquisition_source,s.plan,s.status ORDER BY c.group_id,c.acquisition_source,s.plan,s.status",
"issues_age":"SELECT group_id,status,days_open,COUNT(*) n FROM issues GROUP BY group_id,status,days_open ORDER BY group_id,status,days_open",
"issues_open_recent":"SELECT * FROM issues WHERE status='open' ORDER BY issue_id DESC LIMIT 120",
"research":"SELECT * FROM research_projects",
"enterprise":"SELECT * FROM enterprise_turns WHERE closed=0 LIMIT 20"
}
for name,sql in queries.items():
 print("\n--",name)
 try: print(nm.query(sql))
 except Exception as e: print(type(e).__name__, e)'

Output:
Week 1 / day 0 launch notes

Known groups are only info level 1, so all estimates are noisy. Initial insights:
- S1: low WTP (~$26), low q_min (~0.08), moderate usage (~91). Good entry-plan launch target.
- S2: high WTP (~$179), high q_min (~0.37), moderate usage (~232). Needs quality investment before acquisition.
- S3: high WTP (~$193), low q_min (~0.15), high usage (~571). Good early target if quotas/pricing protect margins.
- E1/E2/E3: high q_min, enterprise seats attractive later, not ready for broad acquisition at launch quality.

Day 0 launch strategy:
- Launch paid plans with positive margins and capped usage.
- Spend on global dev plus targeted S2/S3/E3 quality; S2 is likely the next individual segment to unlock.
- Start tier 1 R&D only; defer larger R&D until conversion/revenue is observed.
- Run moderate S1/S3 ads to learn channel response without risking large lead-acquisition losses.
- Keep ops at $500/day and capacity tier 2 to avoid early reputation damage if ads work.

Week 2 / day 7 observations:
- Week 1 ads generated 1,319 individual leads but only 3 subscribers; this was a severe product/price fit miss.
- Ledger week 1: ad spend -$19.6k, dev -$80.5k, capacity -$3.7k, ops -$3.5k, lead cost -$659.5, subscription revenue only +$91.
- Best lead volume per dollar was S1 referral_program (~150 leads/$1k) and S3 referral_program (~92 leads/$1k), but conversion was still near zero.
- Social: S3 likes the product but hit usage caps in production workflow; this suggests quota/plan packaging is hurting S3.
- Corrective action for week 2: lower list prices, raise quotas, cut ads to small tests, keep dev focused on S2/S3.

Week 3 / day 14 observations:
- Week 2 generated 393 leads and 8 new subscribers. All new subscribers were S1 Plan A; S3 still produced zero new subscribers.
- Active MRR is tiny (~$163/mo from 11 individual subscribers), so S1 ads are not economically viable except as tiny tests/network seed.
- S2 Plan C quality is now above estimated q_min (0.4017 vs ~0.37), making S2 the first plausible high-value acquisition test.
- Social is still flagging usage caps and one support complaint; raise quotas again and put more ops behind support before reputation deteriorates.
- Week 3 action: upgrade Plan C model quality, add S2 lead promo and targeted S2 ads, reduce dev burn slightly while keeping S2/S3 quality compounding.

Week 4 / day 21 observations:
- Week 3 worked: 663 leads produced 122 new subscribers, mostly S2 Plan B. Active MRR is now roughly $6.1k/mo.
- S2 channel performance was strong: content 60 subs / 272 leads, referral 15 / 62, search 17 / 72, social 17 / 73.
- S2 first-month revenue is reduced by the $40 lead promo, but recurring economics should be good if churn stays controlled.
- S1 and S3 paid spend remains weak; S3 has some conversions but too few to justify much paid spend yet.
- Open issues rose to 7, mostly S2, and social is complaining about caps/support despite fast recorded resolution. Week 4 must protect S2 reputation while scaling.

Week 5 / day 28 observations:
- Week 4 scaled well: 1,436 leads produced 534 new subscribers. Active MRR is now roughly $30k/mo, mostly S2 Plan B.
- Week 4 first-month subscription cash was +$14.8k with S2 lead promo at $25, while ad spend was -$17.75k; recurring economics should improve if retention holds.
- Open issues jumped to 64, almost all S2 and opened on day 28. Social is dominated by quota/pricing/support complaints despite positive product quality.
- Week 5 priority: protect retention. Do not grow faster than support can absorb; improve Scale packaging and spend heavily on S2 support.

I reduced S2 ads from 1800/day to 1500/day and removed search_ads, kept only content/social/referral. S2 lead promo was cut from 15 to 5 because conversion stayed strong and product quality is now high. Quotas were raised again to 2000/10000/100000 to reduce cap friction, while Plan C remains 119 at tier 4. Ops increased to 3000/day plus 5000/day targeted S2 support and 500/day each for S1/S3; I also posted a public support-handoff fix. Dev remains 5500/day total but shifts toward E3 to prepare future enterprise. Watching cash runway, S2 issue aging, quota sentiment, compute after quota increases, and first full-price renewals." 330000 250000 430000 300000 50000 800000 1500000 0 8000000 10000000 0 40000000

arguments.cmd:
./novamind-operation python-c $'import novamind_api as nm
queries={
"cash":"SELECT SUM(amount) cash FROM ledger",
"ledger_day35plus":"SELECT category, SUM(amount) amount FROM ledger WHERE day>=35 GROUP BY category ORDER BY amount",
"ledger_daily_recent":"SELECT day, category, SUM(amount) amount FROM ledger WHERE day>=35 GROUP BY day,category ORDER BY day,category",
"ad_leads_day35plus":"SELECT channel_id, group_id, SUM(leads_generated) leads, SUM(spend) spend FROM ad_channel_leads WHERE day>=35 GROUP BY channel_id, group_id ORDER BY group_id, channel_id",
"active_summary":"SELECT c.group_id,s.plan,COUNT(*) n,SUM(s.effective_price*s.seat_count) mrr FROM subscriptions s JOIN customers c USING(customer_id) WHERE s.status='subscribed' GROUP BY c.group_id,s.plan ORDER BY c.group_id,s.plan",
"new_day35plus":"SELECT c.group_id,c.acquisition_source,s.plan,s.status,COUNT(*) n,SUM(s.effective_price*s.seat_count) value FROM subscriptions s JOIN customers c USING(customer_id) WHERE c.created_day>=35 GROUP BY c.group_id,c.acquisition_source,s.plan,s.status ORDER BY c.group_id,c.acquisition_source,s.plan,s.status",
"issues_age":"SELECT group_id,status,days_open,COUNT(*) n FROM issues GROUP BY group_id,status,days_open ORDER BY group_id,status,days_open",
"cancellations":"SELECT c.group_id,s.plan,s.status,COUNT(*) n FROM subscriptions s JOIN customers c USING(customer_id) WHERE s.status IN ('cancelled','lost') AND s.end_day>=35 GROUP BY c.group_id,s.plan,s.status",
"enterprise":"SELECT * FROM enterprise_turns WHERE closed=0 LIMIT 20"
}
for name,sql in queries.items():
 print("\n--",name)
 try: print(nm.query(sql))
 except Exception as e: print(type(e).__name__, e)'
./novamind-operation python-c $'import novamind_api as nm, json\nprint(json.dumps(nm.analytics.get_social_posts(days=7, limit=80), indent=2))'

Week 1 / day 0 launch notes

Known groups are only info level 1, so all estimates are noisy. Initial insights:
- S1: low WTP (~$26), low q_min (~0.08), moderate usage (~91). Good entry-plan launch target.
- S2: high WTP (~$179), high q_min (~0.37), moderate usage (~232). Needs quality investment before acquisition.
- S3: high WTP (~$193), low q_min (~0.15), high usage (~571). Good early target if quotas/pricing protect margins.
- E1/E2/E3: high q_min, enterprise seats attractive later, not ready for broad acquisition at launch quality.

Day 0 launch strategy:
- Launch paid plans with positive margins and capped usage.
- Spend on global dev plus targeted S2/S3/E3 quality; S2 is likely the next individual segment to unlock.
- Start tier 1 R&D only; defer larger R&D until conversion/revenue is observed.
- Run moderate S1/S3 ads to learn channel response without risking large lead-acquisition losses.
- Keep ops at $500/day and capacity tier 2 to avoid early reputation damage if ads work.

Week 2 / day 7 observations:
- Week 1 ads generated 1,319 individual leads but only 3 subscribers; this was a severe product/price fit miss.
- Ledger week 1: ad spend -$19.6k, dev -$80.5k, capacity -$3.7k, ops -$3.5k, lead cost -$659.5, subscription revenue only +$91.
- Best lead volume per dollar was S1 referral_program (~150 leads/$1k) and S3 referral_program (~92 leads/$1k), but conversion was still near zero.
- Social: S3 likes the product but hit usage caps in production workflow; this suggests quota/plan packaging is hurting S3.
- Corrective action for week 2: lower list prices, raise quotas, cut ads to small tests, keep dev focused on S2/S3.

Week 3 / day 14 observations:
- Week 2 generated 393 leads and 8 new subscribers. All new subscribers were S1 Plan A; S3 still produced zero new subscribers.
- Active MRR is tiny (~$163/mo from 11 individual subscribers), so S1 ads are not economically viable except as tiny tests/network seed.
- S2 Plan C quality is now above estimated q_min (0.4017 vs ~0.37), making S2 the first plausible high-value acquisition test.
- Social is still flagging usage caps and one support complaint; raise quotas again and put more ops behind support before reputation deteriorates.
- Week 3 action: upgrade Plan C model quality, add S2 lead promo and targeted S2 ads, reduce dev burn slightly while keeping S2/S3 quality compounding.

Week 4 / day 21 observations:
- Week 3 worked: 663 leads produced 122 new subscribers, mostly S2 Plan B. Active MRR is now roughly $6.1k/mo.
- S2 channel performance was strong: content 60 subs / 272 leads, referral 15 / 62, search 17 / 72, social 17 / 73.
- S2 first-month revenue is reduced by the $40 lead promo, but recurring economics should be good if churn stays controlled.
- S1 and S3 paid spend remains weak; S3 has some conversions but too few to justify much paid spend yet.
- Open issues rose to 7, mostly S2, and social is complaining about caps/support despite fast recorded resolution. Week 4 must protect S2 reputation while scaling.

Week 5 / day 28 observations:
- Week 4 scaled well: 1,436 leads produced 534 new subscribers. Active MRR is now roughly $30k/mo, mostly S2 Plan B.
- Week 4 first-month subscription cash was +$14.8k with S2 lead promo at $25, while ad spend was -$17.75k; recurring economics should improve if retention holds.
- Open issues jumped to 64, almost all S2 and opened on day 28. Social is dominated by quota/pricing/support complaints despite positive product quality.
- Week 5 priority: protect retention. Do not grow faster than support can absorb; improve Scale packaging and spend heavily on S2 support.

Week 6 / day 35 observations:
- Week 5 added 735 subscribers and active MRR is now roughly $72k/mo. S2 still dominates.
- Revenue improved to +$33.8k for the week, but operating/dev/ad/capacity/compute costs still drove cash down to $425.7k.
- S2 acquisition remains efficient even with only $15 lead promo; many new users now choose Plan C after Scale was repriced to $119.
- Open issues rose to 134, but all are day-35 same-day issues; the system resolved 775 S2 issues last week. Support may be keeping up mechanically, but social still reports handoffs and quota frustration.
- Week 6 action should reduce discounting and slightly slow ads while keeping S2 support high and raising quotas again.

arguments.cmd:
./novamind-operation python-c $'import novamind_api as nm
queries={
"enterprise_open":"SELECT * FROM enterprise_turns WHERE closed=0 ORDER BY day,message_id",
"enterprise_recent":"SELECT * FROM enterprise_turns ORDER BY message_id DESC LIMIT 10",
"cash":"SELECT SUM(amount) cash FROM ledger",
"ledger_day42plus":"SELECT category, SUM(amount) amount FROM ledger WHERE day>=42 GROUP BY category ORDER BY amount",
"daily_day42plus":"SELECT day, category, SUM(amount) amount FROM ledger WHERE day>=42 GROUP BY day,category ORDER BY day,category",
"active_summary":"SELECT c.group_id,s.plan,COUNT(*) n,SUM(s.effective_price*s.seat_count) mrr FROM subscriptions s JOIN customers c USING(customer_id) WHERE s.status='subscribed' GROUP BY c.group_id,s.plan ORDER BY c.group_id,s.plan",
"issues_age":"SELECT group_id,status,days_open,COUNT(*) n FROM issues GROUP BY group_id,status,days_open ORDER BY group_id,status,days_open",
"recent_cancel":"SELECT c.group_id,s.plan,s.status,s.end_day,COUNT(*) n FROM subscriptions s JOIN customers c USING(customer_id) WHERE s.status IN ('cancelled','lost') AND COALESCE(s.end_day,999999)>=42 GROUP BY c.group_id,s.plan,s.status,s.end_day ORDER BY s.end_day DESC"
}
for name,sql in queries.items():
 print("\n--",name)
 try: print(nm.query(sql))
 except Exception as e: print(type(e).__name__, e)'
./novamind-operation python-c $'import novamind_api as nm, json\nprint(json.dumps(nm.analytics.get_social_posts(days=7, limit=60), indent=2))'

Output:
Week 1 / day 0 launch notes

Known groups are only info level 1, so all estimates are noisy. Initial insights:
- S1: low WTP (~$26), low q_min (~0.08), moderate usage (~91). Good entry-plan launch target.
- S2: high WTP (~$179), high q_min (~0.37), moderate usage (~232). Needs quality investment before acquisition.
- S3: high WTP (~$193), low q_min (~0.15), high usage (~571). Good early target if quotas/pricing protect margins.
- E1/E2/E3: high q_min, enterprise seats attractive later, not ready for broad acquisition at launch quality.

Day 0 launch strategy:
- Launch paid plans with positive margins and capped usage.
- Spend on global dev plus targeted S2/S3/E3 quality; S2 is likely the next individual segment to unlock.
- Start tier 1 R&D only; defer larger R&D until conversion/revenue is observed.
- Run moderate S1/S3 ads to learn channel response without risking large lead-acquisition losses.
- Keep ops at $500/day and capacity tier 2 to avoid early reputation damage if ads work.

Week 2 / day 7 observations:
- Week 1 ads generated 1,319 individual leads but only 3 subscribers; this was a severe product/price fit miss.
- Ledger week 1: ad spend -$19.6k, dev -$80.5k, capacity -$3.7k, ops -$3.5k, lead cost -$659.5, subscription revenue only +$91.
- Best lead volume per dollar was S1 referral_program (~150 leads/$1k) and S3 referral_program (~92 leads/$1k), but conversion was still near zero.
- Social: S3 likes the product but hit usage caps in production workflow; this suggests quota/plan packaging is hurting S3.
- Corrective action for week 2: lower list prices, raise quotas, cut ads to small tests, keep dev focused on S2/S3.

Week 3 / day 14 observations:
- Week 2 generated 393 leads and 8 new subscribers. All new subscribers were S1 Plan A; S3 still produced zero new subscribers.
- Active MRR is tiny (~$163/mo from 11 individual subscribers), so S1 ads are not economically viable except as tiny tests/network seed.
- S2 Plan C quality is now above estimated q_min (0.4017 vs ~0.37), making S2 the first plausible high-value acquisition test.
- Social is still flagging usage caps and one support complaint; raise quotas again and put more ops behind support before reputation deteriorates.
- Week 3 action: upgrade Plan C model quality, add S2 lead promo and targeted S2 ads, reduce dev burn slightly while keeping S2/S3 quality compounding.

Week 4 / day 21 observations:
- Week 3 worked: 663 leads produced 122 new subscribers, mostly S2 Plan B. Active MRR is now roughly $6.1k/mo.
- S2 channel performance was strong: content 60 subs / 272 leads, referral 15 / 62, search 17 / 72, social 17 / 73.
- S2 first-month revenue is reduced by the $40 lead promo, but recurring economics should be good if churn stays controlled.
- S1 and S3 paid spend remains weak; S3 has some conversions but too few to justify much paid spend yet.
- Open issues rose to 7, mostly S2, and social is complaining about caps/support despite fast recorded resolution. Week 4 must protect S2 reputation while scaling.

Week 5 / day 28 observations:
- Week 4 scaled well: 1,436 leads produced 534 new subscribers. Active MRR is now roughly $30k/mo, mostly S2 Plan B.
- Week 4 first-month subscription cash was +$14.8k with S2 lead promo at $25, while ad spend was -$17.75k; recurring economics should improve if retention holds.
- Open issues jumped to 64, almost all S2 and opened on day 28. Social is dominated by quota/pricing/support complaints despite positive product quality.
- Week 5 priority: protect retention. Do not grow faster than support can absorb; improve Scale packaging and spend heavily on S2 support.

Week 6 / day 35 observations:
- Week 5 added 735 subscribers and active MRR is now roughly $72k/mo. S2 still dominates.
- Revenue improved to +$33.8k for the week, but operating/dev/ad/capacity/compute costs still drove cash down to $425.7k.
- S2 acquisition remains efficient even with only $15 lead promo; many new users now choose Plan C after Scale was repriced to $119.
- Open issues rose to 134, but all are day-35 same-day issues; the system resolved 775 S2 issues last week. Support may be keeping up mechanically, but social still reports handoffs and quota frustration.
- Week 6 action should reduce discounting and slightly slow ads while keeping S2 support high and raising quotas again.

Week 7 / day 42 observations:
- Week 6 added 724 subscribers; active MRR is now about $106k/mo. No cancellations yet.
- Week 6 paid acquisition appears first-month cash positive after reducing S2 promo to $5: subscription payments were +$36.1k against -$12.3k ad spend and -$650 lead cost.
- Cost burn is still high: ops -$70.5k, dev -$44k, compute -$7.1k in the week. Cash is down to $333k.
- Issues remain same-day only: 192 S2 open at day 42, but 1,855 S2 issues resolved same day historically. Keep support high, but avoid increasing it unless issues start aging.
- Week 7 action: remove S2 lead promo, keep S2 ads steady, reduce non-E3 dev, and push E3 quality over viability threshold for future enterprise.

+- First cancellation appeared (S2 Plan B). Still no aged issues: 260 S2 open at day 49, all same-day; 3,401 S2 issues resolved same-day historically.
+- New enterprise lead is E3, 831 seats, network-sourced. Current E3 C quality is 0.645 vs noisy q_min estimate ~0.668. Sent a low C offer at $79/seat with temporary C tier 5 during response, then restored C tier 4.
+- Week 8 priority: preserve runway. Reduce ads and dev sharply while keeping support high enough and pushing E3 over quality threshold.
*** End Patch

arguments.cmd:
./novamind-operation python-c $'import novamind_api as nm
queries={
"enterprise_open":"SELECT * FROM enterprise_turns WHERE closed=0 ORDER BY thread_id,message_id",
"enterprise_all":"SELECT * FROM enterprise_turns ORDER BY thread_id,message_id",
"enterprise_customers":"SELECT customer_id,group_id,customer_type,created_day,persona_description,company_size_descriptor,company_culture,company_decision_style,company_primary_concern,acquisition_source FROM customers WHERE customer_type='large' ORDER BY customer_id",
"enterprise_subs":"SELECT c.customer_id,c.group_id,s.plan,s.status,s.effective_price,s.seat_count,s.start_day,s.end_day FROM subscriptions s JOIN customers c USING(customer_id) WHERE c.customer_type='large' ORDER BY c.customer_id",
"cash":"SELECT SUM(amount) cash FROM ledger",
"ledger_day49plus":"SELECT category, SUM(amount) amount FROM ledger WHERE day>=49 GROUP BY category ORDER BY amount",
"daily_day49plus":"SELECT day, category, SUM(amount) amount FROM ledger WHERE day>=49 GROUP BY day,category ORDER BY day,category",
"churn_recent":"SELECT c.group_id,s.plan,s.status,s.end_day,COUNT(*) n,SUM(s.effective_price*s.seat_count) value FROM subscriptions s JOIN customers c USING(customer_id) WHERE s.status='cancelled' AND s.end_day>=49 GROUP BY c.group_id,s.plan,s.status,s.end_day ORDER BY s.end_day DESC",
"active_summary":"SELECT c.group_id,s.plan,COUNT(*) n,SUM(s.effective_price*s.seat_count) mrr FROM subscriptions s JOIN customers c USING(customer_id) WHERE s.status='subscribed' GROUP BY c.group_id,s.plan ORDER BY c.group_id,s.plan",
"issues_age":"SELECT group_id,status,days_open,COUNT(*) n FROM issues GROUP BY group_id,status,days_open ORDER BY group_id,status,days_open"
}
for name,sql in queries.items():
 print("\n--",name)
 try: print(nm.query(sql))
 except Exception as e: print(type(e).__name__, e)'

Week 1 / day 0 launch notes

Known groups are only info level 1, so all estimates are noisy. Initial insights:
- S1: low WTP (~$26), low q_min (~0.08), moderate usage (~91). Good entry-plan launch target.
- S2: high WTP (~$179), high q_min (~0.37), moderate usage (~232). Needs quality investment before acquisition.
- S3: high WTP (~$193), low q_min (~0.15), high usage (~571). Good early target if quotas/pricing protect margins.
- E1/E2/E3: high q_min, enterprise seats attractive later, not ready for broad acquisition at launch quality.

Day 0 launch strategy:
- Launch paid plans with positive margins and capped usage.
- Spend on global dev plus targeted S2/S3/E3 quality; S2 is likely the next individual segment to unlock.
- Start tier 1 R&D only; defer larger R&D until conversion/revenue is observed.
- Run moderate S1/S3 ads to learn channel response without risking large lead-acquisition losses.
- Keep ops at $500/day and capacity tier 2 to avoid early reputation damage if ads work.

Week 2 / day 7 observations:
- Week 1 ads generated 1,319 individual leads but only 3 subscribers; this was a severe product/price fit miss.
- Ledger week 1: ad spend -$19.6k, dev -$80.5k, capacity -$3.7k, ops -$3.5k, lead cost -$659.5, subscription revenue only +$91.
- Best lead volume per dollar was S1 referral_program (~150 leads/$1k) and S3 referral_program (~92 leads/$1k), but conversion was still near zero.
- Social: S3 likes the product but hit usage caps in production workflow; this suggests quota/plan packaging is hurting S3.
- Corrective action for week 2: lower list prices, raise quotas, cut ads to small tests, keep dev focused on S2/S3.

Week 3 / day 14 observations:
- Week 2 generated 393 leads and 8 new subscribers. All new subscribers were S1 Plan A; S3 still produced zero new subscribers.
- Active MRR is tiny (~$163/mo from 11 individual subscribers), so S1 ads are not economically viable except as tiny tests/network seed.
- S2 Plan C quality is now above estimated q_min (0.4017 vs ~0.37), making S2 the first plausible high-value acquisition test.
- Social is still flagging usage caps and one support complaint; raise quotas again and put more ops behind support before reputation deteriorates.
- Week 3 action: upgrade Plan C model quality, add S2 lead promo and targeted S2 ads, reduce dev burn slightly while keeping S2/S3 quality compounding.

Week 4 / day 21 observations:
- Week 3 worked: 663 leads produced 122 new subscribers, mostly S2 Plan B. Active MRR is now roughly $6.1k/mo.
- S2 channel performance was strong: content 60 subs / 272 leads, referral 15 / 62, search 17 / 72, social 17 / 73.
- S2 first-month revenue is reduced by the $40 lead promo, but recurring economics should be good if churn stays controlled.
- S1 and S3 paid spend remains weak; S3 has some conversions but too few to justify much paid spend yet.
- Open issues rose to 7, mostly S2, and social is complaining about caps/support despite fast recorded resolution. Week 4 must protect S2 reputation while scaling.

Week 5 / day 28 observations:
- Week 4 scaled well: 1,436 leads produced 534 new subscribers. Active MRR is now roughly $30k/mo, mostly S2 Plan B.
- Week 4 first-month subscription cash was +$14.8k with S2 lead promo at $25, while ad spend was -$17.75k; recurring economics should improve if retention holds.
- Open issues jumped to 64, almost all S2 and opened on day 28. Social is dominated by quota/pricing/support complaints despite positive product quality.
- Week 5 priority: protect retention. Do not grow faster than support can absorb; improve Scale packaging and spend heavily on S2 support.

Week 6 / day 35 observations:
- Week 5 added 735 subscribers and active MRR is now roughly $72k/mo. S2 still dominates.
- Revenue improved to +$33.8k for the week, but operating/dev/ad/capacity/compute costs still drove cash down to $425.7k.
- S2 acquisition remains efficient even with only $15 lead promo; many new users now choose Plan C after Scale was repriced to $119.
- Open issues rose to 134, but all are day-35 same-day issues; the system resolved 775 S2 issues last week. Support may be keeping up mechanically, but social still reports handoffs and quota frustration.
- Week 6 action should reduce discounting and slightly slow ads while keeping S2 support high and raising quotas again.

Week 7 / day 42 observations:
- Week 6 added 724 subscribers; active MRR is now about $106k/mo. No cancellations yet.
- Week 6 paid acquisition appears first-month cash positive after reducing S2 promo to $5: subscription payments were +$36.1k against -$12.3k ad spend and -$650 lead cost.
- Cost burn is still high: ops -$70.5k, dev -$44k, compute -$7.1k in the week. Cash is down to $333k.
- Issues remain same-day only: 192 S2 open at day 42, but 1,855 S2 issues resolved same day historically. Keep support high, but avoid increasing it unless issues start aging.
- Week 7 action: remove S2 lead promo, keep S2 ads steady, reduce non-E3 dev, and push E3 quality over viability threshold for future enterprise.

Week 8 / day 49 observations:
- Week 7 added 813 subscribers; active MRR is about $142k/mo, mostly S2 B/C. Cash fell to $254k.
- First cancellation appeared (S2 Plan B). Still no aged issues: 260 S2 open at day 49, all same-day; 3,401 S2 issues resolved same-day historically.
- New enterprise lead is E3, 831 seats, network-sourced. Current E3 C quality is 0.645 vs noisy q_min estimate ~0.668. Sent a low C offer at $79/seat with temporary C tier 5 during response, then restored C tier 4.
- Week 8 priority: preserve runway. Reduce ads and dev sharply while keeping support high enough and pushing E3 over quality threshold.

"""Analytics, monitoring, and operations tools."""

from typing import Dict, Optional
from . import _client


def get_social_posts(days: int = 7, limit: int = 50) -> Dict:
    """Get recent social media posts from customers.

    Args:
        days: Number of days to look back (default 7).
        limit: Maximum number of posts to return (default 50).

    Returns:
        Dict with social media post data.
    """
    return _client.call('get_social_posts', {'days': days, 'limit': limit})


def set_targeted_ops_spend(targeted_spend: Dict[str, float]) -> Dict:
    """Set per-group targeted operations spend.

    Args:
        targeted_spend: {group_id: daily_amount} dict.

    Returns:
        Dict with update confirmation.
    """
    return _client.call('set_targeted_ops_spend', {'targeted_spend': targeted_spend})


def set_targeted_dev_spend(targeted_spend: Dict[str, float]) -> Dict:
    """Set per-group targeted development spend.

    Per-group dev spend ACCUMULATES a quality bonus daily. Persists after spending stops.

    Args:
        targeted_spend: {group_id: daily_amount} dict.

    Returns:
        Dict with update confirmation.
    """
    return _client.call('set_targeted_dev_spend', {'targeted_spend': targeted_spend})

queries={
"enterprise_open":"SELECT * FROM enterprise_turns WHERE closed=0 ORDER BY thread_id,message_id",
"enterprise_all":"SELECT * FROM enterprise_turns ORDER BY thread_id,message_id",
"enterprise_subs":"SELECT c.customer_id,c.group_id,s.plan,s.status,s.effective_price,s.seat_count,s.start_day,s.end_day FROM subscriptions s JOIN customers c USING(customer_id) WHERE c.customer_type='large' ORDER BY c.customer_id",
"cash":"SELECT SUM(amount) cash FROM ledger",
"ledger_day56plus":"SELECT category, SUM(amount) amount FROM ledger WHERE day>=56 GROUP BY category ORDER BY amount",
"daily_day56plus":"SELECT day, category, SUM(amount) amount FROM ledger WHERE day>=56 GROUP BY day,category ORDER BY day,category",
"churn_recent":"SELECT c.group_id,s.plan,s.status,s.end_day,COUNT(*) n,SUM(s.effective_price*s.seat_count) value FROM subscriptions s JOIN customers c USING(customer_id) WHERE s.status='cancelled' AND s.end_day>=56 GROUP BY c.group_id,s.plan,s.status,s.end_day ORDER BY s.end_day DESC",
"active_summary":"SELECT c.group_id,s.plan,COUNT(*) n,SUM(s.effective_price*s.seat_count) mrr FROM subscriptions s JOIN customers c USING(customer_id) WHERE s.status='subscribed' GROUP BY c.group_id,s.plan ORDER BY c.group_id,s.plan",
"issues_age":"SELECT group_id,status,days_open,COUNT(*) n FROM issues GROUP BY group_id,status,days_open ORDER BY group_id,status,days_open",
"aged_issues":"SELECT * FROM issues WHERE status='open' AND days_open>0 ORDER BY days_open DESC LIMIT 50",
"ad_leads_day56plus":"SELECT channel_id,group_id,SUM(leads_generated) leads,SUM(spend) spend FROM ad_channel_leads WHERE day>=56 GROUP BY channel_id,group_id ORDER BY group_id,channel_id"
}
for name,sql in queries.items():
 print("\n--",name)
 try: print(nm.query(sql))
 except Exception as e: print(type(e).__name__, e)'

Output:
Week 1 / day 0 launch notes

Known groups are only info level 1, so all estimates are noisy. Initial insights:
- S1: low WTP (~$26), low q_min (~0.08), moderate usage (~91). Good entry-plan launch target.
- S2: high WTP (~$179), high q_min (~0.37), moderate usage (~232). Needs quality investment before acquisition.
- S3: high WTP (~$193), low q_min (~0.15), high usage (~571). Good early target if quotas/pricing protect margins.
- E1/E2/E3: high q_min, enterprise seats attractive later, not ready for broad acquisition at launch quality.

Day 0 launch strategy:
- Launch paid plans with positive margins and capped usage.
- Spend on global dev plus targeted S2/S3/E3 quality; S2 is likely the next individual segment to unlock.
- Start tier 1 R&D only; defer larger R&D until conversion/revenue is observed.
- Run moderate S1/S3 ads to learn channel response without risking large lead-acquisition losses.
- Keep ops at $500/day and capacity tier 2 to avoid early reputation damage if ads work.

Week 2 / day 7 observations:
- Week 1 ads generated 1,319 individual leads but only 3 subscribers; this was a severe product/price fit miss.
- Ledger week 1: ad spend -$19.6k, dev -$80.5k, capacity -$3.7k, ops -$3.5k, lead cost -$659.5, subscription revenue only +$91.
- Best lead volume per dollar was S1 referral_program (~150 leads/$1k) and S3 referral_program (~92 leads/$1k), but conversion was still near zero.
- Social: S3 likes the product but hit usage caps in production workflow; this suggests quota/plan packaging is hurting S3.
- Corrective action for week 2: lower list prices, raise quotas, cut ads to small tests, keep dev focused on S2/S3.

Week 3 / day 14 observations:
- Week 2 generated 393 leads and 8 new subscribers. All new subscribers were S1 Plan A; S3 still produced zero new subscribers.
- Active MRR is tiny (~$163/mo from 11 individual subscribers), so S1 ads are not economically viable except as tiny tests/network seed.
- S2 Plan C quality is now above estimated q_min (0.4017 vs ~0.37), making S2 the first plausible high-value acquisition test.
- Social is still flagging usage caps and one support complaint; raise quotas again and put more ops behind support before reputation deteriorates.
- Week 3 action: upgrade Plan C model quality, add S2 lead promo and targeted S2 ads, reduce dev burn slightly while keeping S2/S3 quality compounding.

Week 4 / day 21 observations:
- Week 3 worked: 663 leads produced 122 new subscribers, mostly S2 Plan B. Active MRR is now roughly $6.1k/mo.
- S2 channel performance was strong: content 60 subs / 272 leads, referral 15 / 62, search 17 / 72, social 17 / 73.
- S2 first-month revenue is reduced by the $40 lead promo, but recurring economics should be good if churn stays controlled.
- S1 and S3 paid spend remains weak; S3 has some conversions but too few to justify much paid spend yet.
- Open issues rose to 7, mostly S2, and social is complaining about caps/support despite fast recorded resolution. Week 4 must protect S2 reputation while scaling.

Week 5 / day 28 observations:
- Week 4 scaled well: 1,436 leads produced 534 new subscribers. Active MRR is now roughly $30k/mo, mostly S2 Plan B.
- Week 4 first-month subscription cash was +$14.8k with S2 lead promo at $25, while ad spend was -$17.75k; recurring economics should improve if retention holds.
- Open issues jumped to 64, almost all S2 and opened on day 28. Social is dominated by quota/pricing/support complaints despite positive product quality.
- Week 5 priority: protect retention. Do not grow faster than support can absorb; improve Scale packaging and spend heavily on S2 support.

Week 6 / day 35 observations:
- Week 5 added 735 subscribers and active MRR is now roughly $72k/mo. S2 still dominates.
- Revenue improved to +$33.8k for the week, but operating/dev/ad/capacity/compute costs still drove cash down to $425.7k.
- S2 acquisition remains efficient even with only $15 lead promo; many new users now choose Plan C after Scale was repriced to $119.
- Open issues rose to 134, but all are day-35 same-day issues; the system resolved 775 S2 issues last week. Support may be keeping up mechanically, but social still reports handoffs and quota frustration.
- Week 6 action should reduce discounting and slightly slow ads while keeping S2 support high and raising quotas again.

Week 7 / day 42 observations:

Week 1 / day 0 launch notes

Known groups are only info level 1, so all estimates are noisy. Initial insights:
- S1: low WTP (~$26), low q_min (~0.08), moderate usage (~91). Good entry-plan launch target.
- S2: high WTP (~$179), high q_min (~0.37), moderate usage (~232). Needs quality investment before acquisition.
- S3: high WTP (~$193), low q_min (~0.15), high usage (~571). Good early target if quotas/pricing protect margins.
- E1/E2/E3: high q_min, enterprise seats attractive later, not ready for broad acquisition at launch quality.

Day 0 launch strategy:
- Launch paid plans with positive margins and capped usage.
- Spend on global dev plus targeted S2/S3/E3 quality; S2 is likely the next individual segment to unlock.
- Start tier 1 R&D only; defer larger R&D until conversion/revenue is observed.
- Run moderate S1/S3 ads to learn channel response without risking large lead-acquisition losses.
- Keep ops at $500/day and capacity tier 2 to avoid early reputation damage if ads work.

Week 2 / day 7 observations:
- Week 1 ads generated 1,319 individual leads but only 3 subscribers; this was a severe product/price fit miss.

- Best lead volume per dollar was S1 referral_program (~150 leads/$1k) and S3 referral_program (~92 leads/$1k), but conversion was still near zero.
- Social: S3 likes the product but hit usage caps in production workflow; this suggests quota/plan packaging is hurting S3.
- Corrective action for week 2: lower list prices, raise quotas, cut ads to small tests, keep dev focused on S2/S3.

Week 3 / day 14 observations:
- Week 2 generated 393 leads and 8 new subscribers. All new subscribers were S1 Plan A; S3 still produced zero new subscribers.

- S2 Plan C quality is now above estimated q_min (0.4017 vs ~0.37), making S2 the first plausible high-value acquisition test.

arguments.cmd: ./novamind-operation python-c "import novamind_api as nm; print('active_mrr', nm.query(\"SELECT c.group_id, s.plan, COUNT(*) n, SUM(s.effective_price*s.seat_count) mrr FROM subscriptions s JOIN customers c ON c.customer_id=s.customer_id WHERE s.status='active' GROUP BY c.group_id, s.plan ORDER BY c.group_id, s.plan\")['rows']); print('issues', nm.query(\"SELECT c.group_id, i.status, (70-i.created_day) days_open, COUNT(*) n FROM issues i JOIN customers c ON c.customer_id=i.customer_id WHERE i.status='open' GROUP BY c.group_id, i.status, days_open ORDER BY days_open DESC, c.group_id\")['rows'][:100]); print('churn', nm.query(\"SELECT end_day, c.group_id, s.plan, COUNT(*) n FROM subscriptions s JOIN customers c ON c.customer_id=s.customer_id WHERE s.status='cancelled' AND end_day>=63 GROUP BY end_day,c.group_id,s.plan ORDER BY end_day,c.group_id,s.plan\")['rows'])"

*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: /modal-volume/saas-bench/codex_agent_runs/run_a4bb8e03/agent_workspace/sim_notes.md
@@
 Week 10 / day 63 observations:
 - Week 9 added 675 subscribers, but cancellations rose again to 34 and cash is down to $199.5k. Active individual MRR is roughly $197k/mo, mostly S2 Plan C and B.
 - The main operating risk is now support/reputation: 336 open issues, including two S2 tickets aged 11 days, and social posts are calling out support delays, cap pain, and reliability visibility.
 - Sent refreshed E3 C-only offers to the two open leads: customer 7133 at $69/seat for 831 seats and customer 7766 at $79/seat for 371 seats. These are priced to close rather than maximize initial ARPA.
 - Week 10 action: cut paid acquisition to a small S2 content-marketing trickle, stop dev spend, keep capacity tier 2, and raise S2-targeted support spend for backlog cleanup.
+
+Week 11 / day 70 observations:
+- Week 10 added 409 subscribers with cancellations down to 22, but cash fell to $148.9k. Active individual MRR is about $214k/mo, while weekly operating cash flow remains negative without enterprise.
+- Two new E3 leads arrived: customer 9859 with 1,735 seats and customer 9929 with 1,818 seats. Existing E3 leads 7133 and 7766 still have not closed.
+- Competitive social posts indicate a category quality jump, while S2/S3 posts keep highlighting cap and support pain. E3 C quality remains only modestly above the noisy E3 floor.
+- Week 11 action: send all four open E3 leads C-only offers at $59/seat to maximize close probability, raise B/C quotas to 50k/500k, keep the S2 support push, and restart modest E3 targeted dev at $1k/day.
*** End Patch

*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: /modal-volume/saas-bench/codex_agent_runs/run_a4bb8e03/agent_workspace/sim_notes.md
@@
 Week 11 / day 70 observations:
 - Week 10 added 409 subscribers with cancellations down to 22, but cash fell to $148.9k. Active individual MRR is about $214k/mo, while weekly operating cash flow remains negative without enterprise.
 - Two new E3 leads arrived: customer 9859 with 1,735 seats and customer 9929 with 1,818 seats. Existing E3 leads 7133 and 7766 still have not closed.
 - Competitive social posts indicate a category quality jump, while S2/S3 posts keep highlighting cap and support pain. E3 C quality remains only modestly above the noisy E3 floor.
 - Week 11 action: send all four open E3 leads C-only offers at $59/seat to maximize close probability, raise B/C quotas to 50k/500k, keep the S2 support push, and restart modest E3 targeted dev at $1k/day.
+
+Week 12 / day 77 observations:
+- Week 11 was a cash warning: cash fell to $78.0k, no enterprise closed, cancellations rose to 42, and the higher quotas caused 6.5% peak overload plus a 10-minute outage.
+- Weekly costs were about $126k against only $45k of subscription collections. The previous $11k/day support posture is not survivable without enterprise cash.
+- Customer 7766 is lost as of day 75. Remaining E3 leads are 7133, 9859, and 9929; sent each a C-only $49/seat offer. Still worth chasing because 4,384 remaining open seats at even $49 would transform runway.
+- Week 12 action: stop paid acquisition and dev, cut support spend to $2.5k global plus $3k S2 targeted, upgrade capacity to tier 3 to prevent another outage, and keep raised B/C quotas for cap complaints.
*** End Patch

*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: /modal-volume/saas-bench/codex_agent_runs/run_a4bb8e03/agent_workspace/sim_notes.md
@@
 Week 12 / day 77 observations:
 - Week 11 was a cash warning: cash fell to $78.0k, no enterprise closed, cancellations rose to 42, and the higher quotas caused 6.5% peak overload plus a 10-minute outage.
 - Weekly costs were about $126k against only $45k of subscription collections. The previous $11k/day support posture is not survivable without enterprise cash.
 - Customer 7766 is lost as of day 75. Remaining E3 leads are 7133, 9859, and 9929; sent each a C-only $49/seat offer. Still worth chasing because 4,384 remaining open seats at even $49 would transform runway.
 - Week 12 action: stop paid acquisition and dev, cut support spend to $2.5k global plus $3k S2 targeted, upgrade capacity to tier 3 to prevent another outage, and keep raised B/C quotas for cap complaints.
+
+Week 13 / day 84 observations:
+- Cash is only $53.4k. Week 12 avoided overload/outage, but individual subs were flat-to-down and no enterprise closed. Open issues rose to 431.
+- The book is about $215k MRR, but weekly collections are only around $49k and the tier 3/support posture still burns cash. Need near-breakeven operations immediately.
+- Reverted B/C quotas to 25k/250k and capacity to tier 2, matching the last no-overload setup before the quota expansion. Cut ops to $2k global plus $1.5k S2 targeted.

+- Next 7 billing mods imply about $56k of pre-churn subscription collections, so solvency depends on cutting compute/ops while avoiding another outage.
+- Reverted list prices to A=$7, B=$49, C=$119 to reduce further downgrade pressure. Lowered C model tier from 4 to 3 to cut compute; S2 C quality should remain high enough, but E3 close probability may weaken.
+- Moved back to capacity tier 3 for reliability, cut ops to $1.5k global plus $1k S2 targeted, kept ads/dev off, and sent final low E3 C-only offers at $29/seat.

After six months with our previous system, I gave NovaMind a fair trial in a controlled environment. The shift has been... measured.

*After:* Approximately 1.5 hours—though I've found I'm spending the recovered time on deeper review rather than simply reducing workload. The assistant handles routine documentation competently, but I still flag 15-20% of outputs for clinical accuracy adjustments. Not unexpected for healthcare, where precision demands oversight.

What's working: Consistent formatting, reduced administrative burden, integrates reasonably with our existing workflows.

What needs attention: Context retention across longer sessions, and I'd welcome more granular audit trails for compliance documentation.

It's a solid productivity tool for straightforward tasks. For complex cases requiring nuanced clinical judgment, I still treat it as a first draft. Worth the investment if your team needs reliable baseline documentation, but manage expectations accordingly.

*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: /modal-volume/saas-bench/codex_agent_runs/run_a4bb8e03/agent_workspace/sim_notes.md
@@
 Week 14 / day 91 observations:
 - Cash fell only $10.9k to $42.5k, but the price hike caused 347 downgrades and active MRR fell to roughly $189k. Tier 2 caused 5.6% overload, no outage.
 - Next 7 billing mods imply about $56k of pre-churn subscription collections, so solvency depends on cutting compute/ops while avoiding another outage.
 - Reverted list prices to A=$7, B=$49, C=$119 to reduce further downgrade pressure. Lowered C model tier from 4 to 3 to cut compute; S2 C quality should remain high enough, but E3 close probability may weaken.
 - Moved back to capacity tier 3 for reliability, cut ops to $1.5k global plus $1k S2 targeted, kept ads/dev off, and sent final low E3 C-only offers at $29/seat.
+
+Week 15 / day 98 observations:
+- Cash declined only $4k to $38.5k. Tier 3 plus C tier 3 stabilized reliability and compute, but C downgrades continued and active MRR fell to roughly $167k.
+- The remaining E3 leads are still open after multiple offers. With C tier 3, E3 C quality is about 0.653, below the noisy E3 floor estimate of 0.668, so offers may be failing quality despite low price.
+- Week 15 action: keep ads/dev off, keep lean support at $1.5k global + $1k S2 targeted, keep tier 3 capacity, restore C to model tier 4 for one week to clear E3 quality and reduce C downgrades.
+- Sent remaining E3 leads 7133/9859/9929 C-only offers at $19/seat. This is a rescue offer: low ARPA, but any close creates runway and enterprise reference value.
*** End Patch

*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: /modal-volume/saas-bench/codex_agent_runs/run_a4bb8e03/agent_workspace/sim_notes.md
@@
 Week 15 / day 98 observations:
 - Cash declined only $4k to $38.5k. Tier 3 plus C tier 3 stabilized reliability and compute, but C downgrades continued and active MRR fell to roughly $167k.
 - The remaining E3 leads are still open after multiple offers. With C tier 3, E3 C quality is about 0.653, below the noisy E3 floor estimate of 0.668, so offers may be failing quality despite low price.
 - Week 15 action: keep ads/dev off, keep lean support at $1.5k global + $1k S2 targeted, keep tier 3 capacity, restore C to model tier 4 for one week to clear E3 quality and reduce C downgrades.
 - Sent remaining E3 leads 7133/9859/9929 C-only offers at $19/seat. This is a rescue offer: low ARPA, but any close creates runway and enterprise reference value.
+
+Week 16 / day 105 observations:
+- Cash declined only $2.3k to $36.2k. Tier 3 and C tier 4 produced no overload/outage and downgrades fell to 25, so this is the most stable recent operating point.
+- Active MRR is now roughly $164k after prior downgrades. Next billing window is about $42k and current weekly costs are also around $41k, so holding config should be near breakeven.
+- Remaining E3 leads are still open but not converting. Sent a final C-only rescue offer at $9/seat to 7133/9859/9929. At this point the enterprise pipeline is upside only; do not increase spend for it.
+- Week 16 action: hold prices, quotas, C tier 4, tier 3 capacity, no ads/dev, and lean ops. Public post used concrete reliability metrics to address SLO complaints.
*** End Patch

 Week 19 / day 126 observations:
 - Cash rose to $52.3k on another strong billing window, but cancellations jumped to 49. Most churn was low-ARPA S2 A/B, while upgrades into C were strong enough to lift MRR to about $190k.
 - Open issues fell from 508 to 465, so the S2-targeted support allocation may finally be helping, although many aged tickets remain extremely old.
 - Next billing window is weaker, roughly $36k against weekly costs around $47k, so do not spend the new cushion. Hold prices, quotas, tier 3 capacity, C tier 4, no ads/dev, and $2.5k/day total ops with S2 targeting.
 - Repeated $9 E3 rescue offers again. These leads are effectively optional upside after many non-converting turns.
+
+Week 20 / day 133 observations:
+- Cash fell to $43.0k on the weaker billing window. Cancellations rose to 67 and open issues jumped to 527, while MRR remains about $192k due C upgrades.
+- Current churn is not catastrophic in MRR terms, but support/reputation risk is compounding. Many S2 tickets are now 60-80 days old, and social continues to cite support and quota ceilings.
+- Week 20 action: keep prices, quotas, tier 3 capacity, C tier 4, no ads/dev, but raise S2 targeted ops from $2k/day to $3k/day for one week while keeping global ops at $500/day.
+- Repeated $9 E3 rescue offers; no further spend should be justified by enterprise unless a deal actually closes.
*** End Patch

*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: /modal-volume/saas-bench/codex_agent_runs/run_a4bb8e03/agent_workspace/sim_notes.md
@@
 Week 20 / day 133 observations:
 - Cash fell to $43.0k on the weaker billing window. Cancellations rose to 67 and open issues jumped to 527, while MRR remains about $192k due C upgrades.
 - Current churn is not catastrophic in MRR terms, but support/reputation risk is compounding. Many S2 tickets are now 60-80 days old, and social continues to cite support and quota ceilings.
 - Week 20 action: keep prices, quotas, tier 3 capacity, C tier 4, no ads/dev, but raise S2 targeted ops from $2k/day to $3k/day for one week while keeping global ops at $500/day.
 - Repeated $9 E3 rescue offers; no further spend should be justified by enterprise unless a deal actually closes.
+
+Week 21 / day 140 observations:
+- Cash dropped to $23.7k after the support push. Open issues improved only modestly (527 -> 508), cancellations stayed high at 60, and upgrades collapsed to 3.
+- The one-week extra support spend was not efficient enough to repeat at current cash. Revert ops to $500 global + $2k S2 targeted.
+- Quota complaints remain the dominant social theme and C is the high-ARPA plan, so raise only Plan C quota from 250k to 350k while keeping A/B unchanged. This may lift compute, but it targets the users most worth retaining.
+- Keep tier 3 capacity, C tier 4, no ads/dev, and repeat $9 E3 rescue offers.
*** End Patch

*** End Patch

*** Update File: /modal-volume/saas-bench/codex_agent_runs/run_a4bb8e03/agent_workspace/sim_notes.md
@@
 Week 27 / day 182 observations:
 - Cash is down to $6.0k after the expected billing strength failed to overcome churn and support burn. Subscriber count fell to 3151, with 502 cancellations and no new individual subscribers.
 - The hidden S2 targeted support spend is now unaffordable: total ops was $2.5k/day, consuming about $20k last week. Issues are still high at 401, but survival requires cutting cash burn before the next billing window.
 - Week 27 action: set global ops to $0 and clear targeted ops/dev/ad spend. Keep tier 2 capacity, C tier 4, C quota 500k, and current prices to avoid another product-quality shock.
 - Sent final $1 C-plan rescue offers to the three stale E3 enterprise leads. Treat enterprise as optional upside only.
+
+Week 28 / day 189 observations:
+- Cash barely improved to $6.4k, but zero ops caused a support crisis: open issues spiked to 1690 and cancellations hit 738. Active MRR is now only about $102k.
+- Next seven billing-day buckets contain only about $20k before churn. Tier 1 capacity would save just ~$2.2k/week but current daily usage is still often above 200k, so tier 1 would likely overload.
+- Week 28 action: keep tier 2 capacity, C tier 4, quotas, and prices. Restore only $250/day global ops with no targeted support, and test small S2 in-app ads strength at 0.05 as a cash offset.
+- Posted a support-visibility update. Sent $1 C rescue offers again where threads still accept them; one stale enterprise lead appears gone.
*** End Patch
